fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

These reasons for judgment explain the Orders that Court has made staying the operation of three final parenting orders made by this Court on 13 February 2026 (“the final orders”) pending an appeal against the same to the Full Court of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1).

BACKGROUND

The parties are the applicant father (“the applicant”) and the respondent mother (“the respondent”) to a child born in 2020 (“the child”). The parties have been locked in a seemingly intractable conflict since at least July 2021. The final hearing of this matter occurred over two tranches: 3–5 February 2025 and 4–6 August 2025. On 13 February 2026, the final orders were made and reasons for judgment delivered: Bohler & Jugovac (No 3) [2026] FedCFamC1F 83 (“the final reasons”). A Notice of Appeal was filed by the applicant on 18 February 2026 (“the Notice of Appeal”) appealing against specific orders made.
It is not necessary, for present purposes, to traverse the detailed history of these proceedings.

THE STAY APPLICATION

By way of an Application in a Proceeding filed 16 February 2026 (“the stay application”), the applicant seeks to stay Orders five, eight and seventeen of the final orders.
Order five of the final orders permits the respondent to “be at liberty to permanently relocate with the child to Japan”. Order eight of the final orders permits the child to travel overseas and for “the Australian Federal Police give effect to this order by removing the name of the said child from the Family Law Watchlist in force at all points of arrival and departure in the Commonwealth of Australia”. It is apparent that the applicant seeks to challenge the validity of this Court’s decision to allow the respondent and the child to return to Japan.
Order seventeen of the final orders relates to the child “attending upon [her therapist] to continue counselling whilst she resides in the Commonwealth of Australia”.
The applicant also seeks to reinstate the former Family Law Watchlist order placed on the respondent the child, whilst the respondent seeks a number of other financial orders including enforcement of property orders and reimbursement of outstanding day care fees (Amended Response to a Case Application filed 24 February 2026 (“the response”).

THE NOTICE OF APPEAL

The applicant filed the Notice of Appeal on 18 February 2026 in these terms:
His Honour erred at [112] of the judgement, in finding that the Mother’s allegations of stalking were not made out on the evidence, he did not engage with the natural inference which followed, namely that the allegations made by the Mother were exaggerated or fabricated, thus failing to either make an adverse credit finding against the Mother or considering the risk that the Mother would engage in similar conduct upon her relocation.
His Honour erred at [112]in finding that ‘It was also alluded throughout cross-examination that findings of fact may have been sought in relation to conduct which the mother asserts amounted to “stalking” by the father. This was not pressed by any party in their written submissions.’ Where the Father specifically sought findings/ inferences be drawn from the ‘stalking’ incidents.
His Honour erred at [132] in being confident that the Mother would comply with orders for registration of mirror orders upon her return to Japan, as this finding did not take into account the Mother’s efforts to have the Father criminally charged up until as recently as late 2024.
His Honour erred at [181] in finding that ‘there are alternative explanations for (the Mother’s) conduct’ but no alternative explanations were articulated in the judgement nor the evidentiary basis for accepting those explanations.
His Honor erred at [183] in finding that the factor weighed heavily in favour of the Mother’s relocation in circumstances where the Court could only have come to this conclusion after categorically rejecting the evidence of the Single Expert as to the ‘damaging’ nature of relocation and the Father’s allegations of police shopping. Insufficient reasons were given for coming to that conclusion.
His Honour erred at paragraph [191] in finding that the Mother’s conduct of ‘police shopping’ might continue in Japan was baseless. That finding was inconsistent with His Honour’s own finding and the Mother’s evidence that she was hypervigilant. The finding that the Mother’s ‘police shopping’ and subsequent allegations were only to be viewed through the context of the finding made of family violence does not take into account the particulars of each incident of ‘police shopping’.
His Honour erred at [200] in finding that the answers of the single expert lead to the conclusion that relocation ought to occur quickly.
His Honour erred at [201] in finding that the Father failed to establish the benefit to the Child of a delayed relocation in circumstances where the benefit to the Child was adduced during the cross-examination of the Single Expert.
His Honour erred during the course of proceedings in allowing cross examination of the Mother where her demeanour was not visible to those at the bar-table with findings of fact and credit then made based on observations which could not be questioned by the advocates.
Comments made by His Honour during the final hearing as to credibility of the Mother’s account of family violence lead to the perception of apprehended bias.
His Honor was mistaken as to fact at Paragraph [148] in which he stated that there were numerous affidavits before the Court from family, friends and colleagues whereas the only supporting evidence in the Mother’s case was from her family members, as she chose not to read the other affidavits. This mistake was material in that it weakens the evidentiary basis for His Honour’s finding that the Mother had stronger community, social and employment prospects in Japan.
His Honour’s decision to allow the relocation to occur prior to consent orders being made in Japan did not take into account the inherent uncertainty from the Mother’s expert witnesses as to the process which a Japanese Court would need to undertake in order to make those orders. This order lead to the following material errors:
All of the parties sought orders that consent orders be made in Japan to ‘mirror’ orders made in Australia and His Honour did not take submissions from the party as to the possibility that those orders may not be made.
No minute of order was put to the Japanese expert witness to seek advise as to their compatibility with Japanese law.
His Honour did not provide a mechanism for the Child’s return to Australia should the orders not be made in Japan.
His Honour’s judgement was based on the assumption that orders would be made in Japan ‘mirroring’ orders made in Australia.
If His Honour contemplated the possibility that orders would not be made in Japan notwithstanding the best efforts of both parties to have those orders made, no reasons were given for that assumption and to do so would have failed to take into account the risk of noncompliance by the Mother.
The grounds of appeal will be referred to where relevant.

THE EVIDENCE

In support of his case, the applicant relied upon the following material:
(1) The stay application;

(2) His affidavit filed 16 February 2026;

(3) His affidavit filed 24 February 2026; and

(4) Notice of Appeal filed on 18 February 2026.

In support of her case, the respondent relied upon the following material:
(1) The response; and

(2) Her affidavit filed 17 February 2026 (“the respondent’s affidavit”).

THE APPLICABLE LAW

The principles relevant to the present stay application are conveniently set out by the Full Court in Aldridge & Keaton (Stay Appeal) [2009] FamCAFC 106 at [18]:
The principles to be applied in determining an application for a stay of orders both in the general law and in respect of parenting proceedings are also well known. The authorities stress the discretionary nature of the application which should be determined on its merits. Principles relevant to this matter include the following:
the onus to establish a proper basis for the stay is on the applicant for the stay. However it is not necessary for the applicant to demonstrate any “special” or “exceptional” circumstances;
a person who has obtained a judgment is entitled to the benefit of that judgment;
a person who has obtained a judgment is entitled to presume the judgment is correct;
the mere filing of an appeal is insufficient to grant a stay;
the bona fides of the applicant;
a stay may be granted on terms that are fair to all parties - this may involve a court weighing the balance of convenience and the competing rights of the parties;
a weighing of the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory if a stay is not granted – this will be a substantial factor in determining whether it will be appropriate to grant a stay;
some preliminary assessment of the strength of the proposed appeal – whether the appellant has an arguable case;
the desirability of limiting the frequency of any change in a child’s living arrangements;
the period of time in which the appeal can be heard and whether existing satisfactory arrangements may support the granting of the stay for a short period of time; and
the best interests of the child the subject of the proceedings are a significant consideration.

(Citations omitted)

THE PARTIES CASES

The applicant sought the following orders within the stay application:
1. That this application be heard on an urgent basis;
That Order 5 made by His Honour Justice Altobelli on 13 February 2026 be stayed;

3. That Order 17 made by His Honour Justice Altobelli on 13 February 2026 be

stayed;
That Order 8 made by His Honor [sic] Justice Altobelli on 13 February 2026 be stayed and;
That until further Order, each party, [Mr Boehler] born [in] 1982 and [Ms Jugovac] born [in] 1987, their servants and/or agents be and are hereby restrained from removing or attempting to remove or causing or permitting the removal of the said child [X], born [in] 2020 (“the child”) from the Commonwealth of Australia, AND IT IS REQUESTED that the Australian Federal Police give effect to this order by placing the name of the said child on the Airport Watch List in force at all points of arrival and departure in the Commonwealth of Australia and maintain the child’s name on the Watch List until the Court orders its removal.
The Court notes that it made an interim Family Law Watchlist Orders of the kind sought above on 15 February 2026.
The applicants’ primary submissions in favour of the stay application are:
That there is a proper basis for the stay;
That there is a real risk of the appeal being rendered nugatory if the stay application is not granted;
That the appeal has sufficient prospects of success to justify a maintenance of the status quo;
That the grant of the stay, and a continuation of the parenting arrangements in accordance with the Orders of this Court dated 18 February 2025 (“the interim orders”) will limit the frequency of change in the child’s living arrangements; and
That in circumstances where the appeal is listed for hearing on 28 April 2026, the balance of convenience favours the grant of a stay application.
The respondent sought the following orders in the response:
1. That the Applicant's interim application be dismissed

If that is not accepted,
That the Applicant's interim application be adjourned until such time as the Applicant has fulfilled his obligations under the Orders of 13 February 2025, and pay to the Respondent $30,000 plus lost interest.
That the Respondent be at liberty to travel to Japan from [...] March 2026 to [...] April 2026 , and from [...] July to [...] July 2026.
That the Applicant spend school holiday time with the Child on 17, 18 and 19 April 2026
That from the commencement of 21 April 2026 being Term 2, the Child live with the Mother and spend time with the Father as agreed between the parties in writing or as follows:
In Week 1 from the conclusion of school or 3:00pm Friday to the commencement of school or 9:00am Monday.
In Week 2 from the conclusion of school or 3:00pm Wednesday to the commencement of school or 9:00am Thursday.
That the Father pay to the Mother the sum of $1,860 for outstanding day care fees.
That if the Father is unable to pay any of the above sums, that the Father’s investment property located at [Suburb E], NSW be sold, and the proceeds of sale be distributed to the Mother, limited to the total sum in the Orders herein.

8. It is noted that:
That the above school term schedule has been in place from Term 1 which commenced on 02 February 2026, and that the first week of Term 2 falls on the scheduled Week 2 with the Child spending time with the Father from the conclusion of school or 3:00pm Wednesday the 22 April 2026 to the commencement of school or 9:00am Thursday the 23 April 2026 and each alternate week thereafter.
The respondent’s primary submissions against the grant of the stay application are:
That she is entitled to the benefit of the final orders until they are otherwise set aside or discharged;
That she is entitled to presume that the final orders will not be set aside for incorrectness; and
That it is in the best interests of the child to allow the respondent and the child to relocate to Japan.

DISCUSSION

Orders are made with the expectation they will be complied with. To enforce or refuse to enforce an order is discretionary (Ramsey & Ramsey [1982] FamCA 42; (1983) FLC 91-301). It is thus only through an analysis of the respective strength of each factor plead in support of, and in opposition to, the stay application that his Court can made any proper decision.

A person who has obtained a judgment is entitled to presume the judgment is correct

In Australian Coal and Shale Employees’ Federation v The Commonwealth [1953] HCA 25; (1953) 94 CLR 621 at 627, the High Court stated that:
...the true principle limiting the manner in which appellate jurisdiction is exercised in respect of decisions involving discretionary judgment is that there is a strong presumption in favour of the correctness of the decision appealed from, and that that decision should therefore be affirmed unless the court of appeal is satisfied that it is clearly wrong. A degree of satisfaction sufficient to overcome the strength of the presumption may exist where there has been an error which consists in acting upon a wrong principle, or giving weight to extraneous or irrelevant matters, or failing to give weight or sufficient weight to relevant considerations, or making a mistake as to the facts. Again, the nature of the error may not be discoverable, but even so it is sufficient that the result is so unreasonable or plainly unjust that the appellate court may infer that there has been a failure properly to exercise the discretion which the law reposes in the court of first instance.
In the New South Wales Court of Appeal decision Alexander v Cambridge Credit Corporation (1985) 2 NSWLR 685 the Court at 694 cited with approval the decision of Mahoney JA in Re Middle Harbour Investments Ltd (In Liq) (Court of Appeal, 15 December 1976, unreported):
... Prima facie, a successful party is entitled to the benefit of the judgment obtained by him and is entitled to commence with the presumption that the judgment is correct. These are not matters of rigid principle and a court asked to grant a stay will consider each case upon its merits, but where an applicant for a stay has not demonstrated an appropriate case but has left the situation in the state of speculation or of mere argument, weight must be given to the fact that the judgment below has been in favour of the other party.
This principle was accepted by both parties and is inarguable. This factor weighs in favour of dismissing the stay application.

The mere filing of an appeal is insufficient to grant a stay

This principle is also self-evident, was accepted by both parties and is not a determinative issue in this case.

A stay may be granted on terms that are fair to all parties, and this may involve a Court weighing the balance of convenience and competing rights of the parties

The balance of convenience is a technical term which describes the evaluative decision undertaken by a judicial officer in weighing up, “whether the inconvenience or injury which the plaintiff would be likely to suffer if an injunction were refused outweighs or is outweighed by the injury which the defendant would suffer if an injunction were granted” (Beecham Group Ltd v Bristol Laboratories Pty Ltd (1968) 118 CLR 618 at 622-623 (“Beecham Group Ltd”)).
Noting the history of this case, both parties would contend that their respective injury would be greater should their application, or response, be respectively allowed or dismissed. The respondent is understandably keen to return to Japan as soon as reasonably practicable to provide herself and the child with what she believes to be greater social and familial support, access to medical care and options for stable employment. The applicant opposes any relocation to Japan for fear that it would irreparably damage his relationship with the child.
A stay might be granted on terms and conditions which would be fair to all parties. The Court is permitted to make a stay order on conditions (Newett & Newett (No 6) [2021] FamCA 436 at [35]).
The respondent suggests that if any stay is granted, it should be done on terms that allow her to travel with the child to Japan for a holiday. More shall be said on this point below as there is a possibility that the appeal may be rendered nugatory if this is granted.
The respondent proposes that the hearing of the stay be adjourned until the applicant “has fulfilled his obligations under the Orders of 13 February 2025, and pay to the respondent $30,000 plus lost interest” (the response, Order 2), that the applicant pay to the respondent “the sum of $1,860 for outstanding day care fees” (the response, Order 6), and that “if the [applicant] is unable to pay any of the above sums, that the [applicant’s] investment property located at [Suburb E], NSW be sold, and the proceeds of sale be distributed to the [respondent], limited to the total sum in the Orders herein” (the response, Order 7).
The respondent was unable to identify the basis upon which the Court was said to have jurisdiction or power to order such exceptional relief as a condition of the granting of a stay pending an appeal, especially in circumstances where the final orders were limited only to the parenting dispute between the parties.
As this Court stated in Dajani & Dajani (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1F 749 at [26], albeit in the context of the stay of financial proceedings:
The Court’s powers to grant conditions when granting a stay cannot be a power at large. It must be limited by reference to the power exercised when the orders were made in respect of which a stay is now sought... Moreover, if the exercise of the said power is exhausted, as the Court believes was the case... there is no power to make any further order by way of conditions on a stay outside of the parameters of that order (White & White [2014] FamCAFC 213 at [9]–[11])
In relation to the orders for sale of the applicant’s property, and in the context of existing (but not complied with) orders under s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), and further in the absence of any application under s 79A of the Act, the Court concludes that its jurisdiction under s 79 of the Act has been extinguished.
The non-compliance with orders made under s 79 of the Act is not a ground for denying the applicant relief he may be entitled to in parenting proceedings. As was stated by the Full Court in Watson & Watson [2013] FamCAFC 25; (2013) FLC 93-530, leaning on the principles in Hadkinson v Hadkinson [1952] P. 285, at [27]:
In Fahmi, the Full Court carefully traced, by reference to the authorities examined, the history of the rule at common law expressed as “that a party in contempt will not be heard”. The Full Court referenced authorities demonstrating that the scope of the rule’s application from its origin came to be restricted over time. From the position that a person in contempt would not be heard in that or any other proceeding, except if the Court “of special grace suspend the contempt”, importantly the rule came to be restricted by the Court confining its operation to contempt in the same cause or in the same proceedings as that in which the application was made. Further, the Full Court identified the progression from the rule being regarded as a strict rule subject only to specific exceptions to, at least in some jurisdictions, a discretionary rule. That is, as a rule giving rise to a discretion not to hear the party in contempt in the same cause or in the same proceedings as that in which the application was made.

(Emphasis added).
The seminal Full Court decision of Fahmi & Fahmi [1995] FamCAFC 106; (1995) FLC 92-637 at 82,428 affirmed that:
The question, however, for us in this case is whether an application for dissolution of marriage can be said to be in the same cause, or in the same proceedings, as the proceedings between the parties to the marriage in question, in which orders for property settlement, or in which orders for child maintenance have been made. We consider that the answer to this question must be no.
It is not open, as a matter of discretion, for this Court to refuse the applicant a stay of orders where they fall within the course of different “proceedings”, notwithstanding the fact that they may arise from the same martial cause.

The bona fides of the applicant

In submissions made on behalf of the respondent, no contention was made that the appeal lacked bona fides. The Court agrees.

Some preliminary assessment of the strength of the appeal

A relevant consideration in a stay application is whether there is some prospect that the appeal will succeed and where the balance of convenience lies (Jennings Construction Ltd v Burgundy Royale Investments Pty Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 84; (1986) 161 CLR 681). The balance of convenience has been discussed above.
This factor can otherwise be drawn back to its jurisprudential roots in Beecham Group Ltd at 622 where it was said that a Court in making an interlocutory injunction, such as a stay of proceedings, must assess “whether the plaintiff has made out a prima facie case, in the sense that if the evidence remains as it is there is a probability that at the trial of the action the plaintiff will be held entitled to relief”. In Australia Broadcasting Corporation v O’Neil [2006] HCA 46; (2006) 227 CLR 57 the majority accepted at [65] the view that “By using the phrase ‘prima facie case’, their Honours did not mean that the plaintiff must show that it is more probable than not that at trial the plaintiff will succeed; it is sufficient that the plaintiff show a sufficient likelihood of success to justify in the circumstances the preservation of the status quo pending the trial”. This is a lower standard that is more readily understood as suggesting that the Court must determine if there is a serious question to be tried.
There are obvious problems with the Notice of Grounds of Appeal as presently drafted. The solicitor for the applicant was cognisant of this. It was prepared quickly, in circumstances where the applicant feared that the respondent would return to Japan with the child before a watchlist order, or stay, could be ordered.
It is not appropriate to categorise the litany of issues which pervade the grounds of appeal as presently drafted. The Court accepts that the grounds will be reconsidered, and the Notice amended. The applicant should receive the benefit of the doubt in this regard.

Weighing the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory should a stay not be granted

This is perhaps the predominant factor in this stay application. It is, not fancifully, contended that “there is a real risk that it will not be possible for a successful appellant to be restored substantially to his former position if the judgment against him is executed” (Federal Commissioner of Taxation v Myer Emporium Ltd (No 1) [1986] HCA 13; (1986) 160 CLR 220 at 223).
Harper J observed in Samaras & Allen [2021] FedCFamC1F 20 at [39] that:
... The High Court has several times indicated that the enjoyment of a right is rendered “nugatory” if it is rendered worthless or seriously undermined: Byrne v Australian Airlines Ltd (1995) 185 CLR 410 at 450, referring to Nullagine Investments Pty Ltd v Western Australian Club Inc [1993] HCA 45; (1993) 177 CLR 635 at 647-648, 659.
The Court is concerned that, should the respondent and child be allowed to return to Japan, even for a short holiday as she proposes, but the respondent then resists their return to the Commonwealth of Australia; even in the face of a mandatory order to that effect, that this will prejudice the prompt prosecution of, or certainly render more complex, the hearing and determination of the applicant’s appeal.
The submissions made in support of orders enabling the respondent to travel to Japan with the child before the hearing of the appeal did not satisfactorily explain why the travel could not take place after the hearing of the appeal, given the prompt hearing of the appeal which will be discussed further below.
When both parties were asked to address the Court about their compliance with Order 12 of the final orders (which is not the subject of appeal) which deals with registration and enforcement of the final orders in Japan, and even recognising that the final date for compliance with Order 12 was not until 27 February 2026 (two days after the hearing of the stay application), it became clear to the Court that strict compliance was highly unlikely within the timeframe ordered.
The significance of this is in the scenario where litigation in Japan becomes necessary as a result of the mother declining to return to the Commonwealth of Australia. The mother proposes travelling to Japan from March 2026 – April 2026. It is highly unlikely, on the information presently before the Court, that the parties will have succeeded in registering the final orders, let alone commenced the process of registering mirror orders, by the time the mother travels to Japan. Moreover, the inevitable complexity in this context of certain orders being stay pending an appeal, and the appeal itself, adds an unacceptable level of complexity and ambiguity which magnifies the potential consequences of allowing the mother to travel to Japan, even for a short period, should she decide not to return.
The Court is satisfied that the appeal would be rendered nugatory should a stay not be granted.

The desirability of limiting the frequency of any change in a child’s living arrangements

This factor has limited relevance to the present proceedings if the child is not allowed to travel with her mother to Japan, pending the appeal. The previous factor identified above contra‑indicates allowing this travel.
Hypothetically, however, if the child were permitted to travel it would represent a significant change in her living arrangements, even for the four-week period proposed by the respondent. The child is presently enjoying frequent time with the applicant.
If the respondent then declined to return the child, there would be further interruption to the consistency of her parenting arrangements. If the child were then to be returned to the Commonwealth of Australia, either voluntarily or pursuant to Court order, there would again be a further interruption to her parenting arrangements.
The Court finds that the grant of the stay, on the facts of this case, limits the frequency of changes to the child’s living arrangements, insofar as they relate to the father.

The period of time in which the appeal can be heard and whether existing satisfactory arrangements may support the granting of the stay for a short period of time

The appeal is listed for hearing on 28 April 2026, approximately two months from the date on which the stay application was heard. Not only is there little to no complaint by the parties about the existing parenting arrangements, but the Court was invited to make consent orders which, in effect, progress the applicant’s time with the child. Whilst the respondent’s desire to return to Japan is understandable, given all the circumstances of this case, the operative time of any stay granted is measured in potentially 3–4 months, allowing the Full Court a generous time to provide written reasons.
The Court observed no issue with the consent orders proposed by the parties, and views that it is appropriate to make those orders at this juncture.
On these bases, this factor weighs in favour of allowing the stay application.

The best interests of the child

This Court views, as expressed in the final reasons, that it would be in the best interest of the child for both the respondent and the child to relocate to Japan in a reasonably expedient manner. Nothing advanced in the Notice of Appeal, the stay application, nor the applicant’s affidavit convinces this Court otherwise.
This factor, viewed in isolation, weighs against the grant of the stay application.

Other factors relevant to the exercise of discretion

Nothing else was said in submissions relevant to the exercise of discretion.

ORDERS

This Court is satisfied, on balance, that a proper basis for a stay is established for the reasons articulated above. None of the conditions on the stay sought by the respondent have merit.
Normally, the Court would impose a condition that the applicant diligently pursue the appeal, but in circumstances where an appeal hearing date has been allocated, this is unnecessary.
